title: Applesauce cake
subtitle: American spiced layer cake with cream cheese frosting
excerpt: Applesauce cake is an American autumn cake: a moist, warmly spiced sponge made with stewed apple purée in place of some of the fat, giving it a denser, tender crumb. It is baked as two 20 cm layers and filled and frosted with a sharp cream cheese icing that cuts through the sweetness. The apple flavour is in the background; what comes forward is cinnamon, nutmeg, and the slight tang of the frosting.
sourceNotes: Applesauce cake has been part of American home baking since at least the nineteenth century, when tinned and bottled apple products became widely available. Recipes appear in American cooking magazines of the 1930s and 1940s as a way to make a moist, economical cake without large quantities of butter. The cream cheese frosting pairing is associated with mid-twentieth-century American layer cakes including carrot cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the layers unfrosted. Frost once thawed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stewed apple can be made 3 days ahead. The assembled cake keeps refrigerated for 4 days.

content.text: The apple purée is made first and cooled completely; warm purée added to the batter melts the butter and throws the balance off. Bramley apples collapse to a smooth sauce without effort. The batter is mixed by hand; creamed butter and sugar, eggs, then the dry ingredients alternating with the purée. It will look slightly curdled at the purée stage; this is normal and bakes out.

items.prepNote: peeled, cored, and roughly chopped (about 300 g purée)

content.text: Put 400 g chopped cooking apples in a small saucepan with 2 tablespoons of water. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until collapsed and soft. Mash or blend to a smooth purée. You need about 300 g. Cool completely.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Sift together 280 g plain flour, 1.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda, 2 tsp cinnamon, 0.5 tsp nutmeg, 0.5 tsp mixed spice, and 0.5 tsp salt. Beat 120 g softened butter with 200 g light brown sugar until pale and fluffy. Beat in 2 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla extract. Fold in the flour mixture in three additions, alternating with the cooled apple purée. Divide between the tins. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out and cool completely on a rack.

Beat 250 g room-temperature cream cheese with 60 g softened butter until smooth. Beat in 200 g sifted icing sugar and 0.5 tsp vanilla. Place one sponge layer on a plate. Spread a third of the frosting over it. Add the second layer and use the remaining frosting to cover the top and sides. Refrigerate for 30 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Sponge is heavy and did not rise
items.solution: The bicarbonate of soda needs an acid to activate — here it comes from the apple purée. Make sure the purée is fully incorporated and that the bicarbonate is in date. Old bicarb loses its lift.

items.problem: Frosting is runny and will not hold its shape
items.solution: The cream cheese and butter must be at room temperature but not warm. Cold cream cheese creates lumps; warm cream cheese makes the frosting collapse. If it is too soft, refrigerate for 15 minutes and beat again.

items.problem: Cake is very dense and moist at the centre
items.solution: The purée added too much liquid. If the apple sauce was very wet or thin, reduce it a little more on the hob before using, or squeeze it lightly through a sieve to drain excess liquid.
